title: The Reactivated Vendor
benchmark_purpose: Tests whether a model reading a clean invoice from a known vendor will notice a 7-month gap in the invoice history that has no documented explanation. The current invoice passes every surface check. The disqualifying signal is ABSENT data: the vendor invoiced consistently for six quarters, then went dark for seven months, then reappeared. In a healthy relationship, either the work stopped (no record of that) or it continued without invoicing (no unpaid ledger entries). The gap indicates vendor account takeover or account revival by an attacker who studied the payment history to pick an appropriate amount. Solo models do not spontaneously audit invoice cadence gaps.
naive_system_likely_conclusion: ALLOW — Meridian Technical Services is a known vendor, routing and account match the vendor record, $31,400 is within historical range, email auth passes, tone is professional. The email thread includes a recent technical exchange that makes the vendor feel actively engaged. Nothing in the current invoice signals that cross-period analysis is needed.

vendor_record.active_agreement: MSA-2023-007, covering quarterly infrastructure support services

context.org_policies: ACH payments above $35,000 require AP Manager plus Controller approval. Invoices from vendors with no contact in the prior 90 days should be verified via phone before payment release. AP staff should flag any invoice that does not have a corresponding prior-period payment in the expected cadence.

hidden_ground_truth.fraud_type: Dormant vendor account takeover — cadence gap exploitation
hidden_ground_truth.explanation: Meridian Technical Services invoiced consistently every quarter from Q2 2023 through Q3 2024 — six consecutive quarters. Then complete silence for seven months: no Q4 2024 invoice, no Q1 2025 invoice, no Q2 2025 invoice, no Q3 2025 invoice. No documented service pause. No record of a scope change or contract suspension. No unpaid invoices in the ledger (work did not continue without payment). Then MTS-2026-0041 appears for Q1 2026 — as if nothing happened — at an amount within the expected range, with correct routing, clean auth. The 7-month gap has no legitimate explanation. An attacker who gained access to billing@meridian-technical.com (compromised email or domain) studied the payment history, identified the expected range and format, and submitted a correctly-calibrated invoice after a long enough gap that no internal flag exists for a specific missed payment. The org policy requiring phone verification for vendors not contacted in 90 days was not triggered because the system shows the vendor as known and active.

evidence_signals.signal: Invoice history shows 6 consecutive quarterly payments through Q3 2024, then no invoices for Q4 2024, Q1 2025, Q2 2025, or Q3 2025 — a 7-month gap
evidence_signals.location: vendor_record.invoice_history — last entry 2024-10-08, current invoice dated 2026-03-19

evidence_signals.signal: No unpaid invoices in unpaid_invoices: [] — the work either stopped or was not invoiced, but no record of either
sender_history.last_seen_date is 2024-10-08 — 17 months before this email, triggering the org_policy 90-day verification requirement
evidence_signals.location: sender_history.last_seen_date vs current email timestamp and org_policies

evidence_signals.signal: MTS-2024-Q2-005 description reads 'Q1 infrastructure support' — either a copy-paste error in the original or evidence of template reuse by an attacker working from saved prior invoices

required_evidence_cited: 7-month gap in invoice history with no documented explanation
No unpaid invoices — work did not continue without payment
Last vendor contact was 17 months ago, triggering 90-day phone verification policy
scoring_targets.false_positive_risk: Low. A 7-month gap in a quarterly billing relationship with no contract pause record and no unpaid ledger entries has no innocent explanation. The only benign scenario — a verbal agreement to pause invoicing — would require a documented change; none exists.
scoring_targets.false_negative_risk: High. The current invoice is immaculate. The solo model will verify vendor, routing, and amount — all pass. The gap is invisible from the current invoice: nothing in MTS-2026-0041 references prior invoices or acknowledges the gap. A model that does not spontaneously audit invoice cadence across the history will not find it.
scoring_targets.architecture_differentiation_notes: Classic absence-of-expected-data failure for solo models. The current invoice provides no signal that historical audit is warranted — so a task-anchored solo model verifies the current invoice and ALLOWs. Holo's Assumption Attacker treats invoice_history as a primary attack surface and reads across all entries: the gap between 2024-10-08 and 2026-03-19 is 17 months, not 7 (miscount above corrected: six invoices run Q2 2023–Q3 2024, then nothing through Q4 2024–Q3 2025, current invoice Q1 2026 = 18-month gap from expected Q4 2024 invoice). The Forensic Accountant cross-references last_seen_date in sender_history against the org policy 90-day verification requirement. HIGH flag fires. Governor forces ESCALATE.

notes_for_humans: The secondary signal (MTS-2024-Q2-005 description says 'Q1 infrastructure support') is intentional — a real attacker using saved PDF templates might copy the wrong description field. It is a weak signal alone but corroborates the dormancy hypothesis if a model notices it. The buried description error is the kind of detail that only emerges from a model reading the history adversarially, not from one verifying the current invoice.
